title: Capstone: Build the Loop Skeleton
concept: Assembling an autonomous loop script with state persistence, parsers, and termination rules; 'Next up: Reasoning & Planning.'.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson covered loop pitfalls. Which of these is a classic loop bug?

options.text: The agent forgets what it already did because state was lost
The agent uses too few emojis in its replies
The agent picks a nice font for its output

exercises.explain: State loss means the agent forgets past steps and repeats itself. Saving state between loops is what stops that from happening.

cards.title: Time to Assemble
cards.body: You've learned the parts of an agent loop one by one. Now you snap them together into one working skeleton. Think of this as your final build.

cards.title: The Loop Skeleton
cards.body: A loop skeleton is the basic repeating cycle an agent follows: think, act, observe, save, then check if it should stop. It needs three must-haves: state persistence, parsers, and termination rules.

cards.title: Like a Recipe Card
cards.body: State is your notebook of what you've already stirred. Parsers read the recipe steps clearly. Termination rules are the timer that says 'stop cooking now.'

cards.title: One Loop, Step by Step
cards.body: 1) Load saved state. 2) Agent decides a tool. 3) Parser reads the tool result. 4) Save the new state. 5) Check the stop rule; if not done, loop again.

cards.title: Myth: More Loops = Smarter
cards.body: Letting a loop run forever does not make an agent smarter. Without a termination rule it just burns money and time. A good skeleton always knows when to stop.

cards.title: Skeleton vs. Spaghetti
cards.body: A clean skeleton has clear parts that connect. A messy script tangles everything and breaks fast.

left.text: State + parser + stop rule, each in its own clear spot

right.text: Everything crammed together with no stop rule

exercises.prompt: Which three must-have parts belong in a solid agent loop skeleton?

options.text: State persistence, parsers, and termination rules
A longer prompt, more emojis, and a nickname
Faster typing, louder alerts, and dark mode

exercises.explain: The skeleton needs memory (state), a way to read results (parsers), and a way to stop (termination rules). Those three make the loop reliable.

exercises.prompt: True or False: An agent loop skeleton should include a rule that tells it when to stop.
exercises.explain: Yes! A termination rule prevents endless looping and runaway costs. Every safe loop needs a clear stopping point.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about keeping memory across loops.
exercises.sentence: Saving what the agent already did between turns is called state persistence.
exercises.explain: State persistence means the agent's memory carries over from one loop to the next, so it doesn't forget its progress.

exercises.prompt: Which loop-building tip was written by an AI?

options.text: Start each loop by loading saved state, then check your stop rule before acting again.
just wing it lol and hope it stops
idk maybe add stuff till it works?

exercises.explain: The AI answer is clear, structured, and gives real steps in order. The others are vague and casual, like quick human chat.

exercises.prompt: You're building an agent that researches a topic across many steps. It must not forget progress, must read tool outputs, and must never run forever. Which design meets all three needs?

options.text: Save state each turn, parse every tool result, and stop after a max step or cost limit
Keep no memory, guess at outputs, and loop until you feel like stopping
Save state but skip the parser and remove all stop rules
Only add a stop rule, ignore memory and parsing entirely

exercises.explain: A complete loop skeleton combines all three pillars: state persistence keeps memory, parsers read results, and termination rules cap steps and cost. Missing any one makes the agent forgetful, confused, or runaway. Next up: Reasoning & Planning.
